summary: DeVault argues that choosing the right language, framework, or toolset for a project is one of the most consequential and difficult-to-reverse decisions in software development. He advocates for detaching personal familiarity from the decision-making process and being willing to learn new tools when they better fit the problem. Drawing on his own experience across C#, C, Assembly, Python, and other languages, he shows how each choice opened doors to subsequent projects. The post strikes a balance between advocating for learning new tools and acknowledging that familiar ones are sometimes the right choice.
key_points: Language and framework choices are among the hardest decisions to change once made, yet they don't get the respect they deserve
New projects are the only effective way to truly learn a new language, and that learning opportunity shouldn't be squandered
Familiarity with a tool should be detached from the decision of whether to use it — judge tools on fitness for the problem
Each language learned compounds: Python led to sr.ht, C led to Sway and aerc, and each expanded his capabilities for future work
Sometimes new tools don't work out — he rewrote a Rust prototype in C — and that's fine
money_quotes: These are some of the only choices you'll make that you cannot change.
A new project is a great means of learning a new language or framework - the only effective means, in fact.
Why squander the opportunity to choose something unfamiliar when you have the rare opportunity to start working on a new project?
I'm suggesting that you detatch your familiarity with your tools from the decision-making process.
I just threw away and rewrote a prototype in C after deciding that it wasn't a good candidate for Rust.
The skills you already know make for a great hammer, but you shouldn't nail screws to the wall.
key_insight: Detach familiarity from your tooling decisions — choosing the right tool over the comfortable one compounds into skills and projects that wouldn't otherwise exist.
